questions.question: Given a value of type `Bird | Fish`, which members can you access **without** narrowing?
options: Only members present on every union member (the intersection of their member sets)
All members of both `Bird` and `Fish` (the combined member set)
Only the members that are unique to `Bird`
No members at all until you cast the value to a member type
questions.explanation: On a bare union the compiler only exposes members guaranteed on every branch, since at runtime the value could be either type. Accessing the combined set is what an intersection gives you, not a union.

questions.question: What does the type `string & number` evaluate to?
options: `never`
`unknown`
`string | number`
`any`
questions.explanation: No single value can be both a string and a number, so the intersection is empty — the bottom type `never`. `unknown` is the identity element of intersection, not the result of a conflicting one.

questions.question: What are the identity elements of union and intersection, respectively (the types that leave `T` unchanged)?
options: `never` for union (`T | never` = `T`); `unknown` for intersection (`T & unknown` = `T`)
`unknown` for union (`T | unknown` = `T`); `never` for intersection (`T & never` = `T`)
`any` for both union and intersection
`null` for union; `undefined` for intersection
questions.explanation: `T | never` collapses to `T` (never adds no new values), and `T & unknown` collapses to `T` (unknown adds no constraint). This swap is exactly why the `never`-based exhaustiveness pattern works.

questions.question: In JavaScript (and therefore for `typeof` narrowing), `typeof null` evaluates to `"object"`, so `typeof x === "object"` does **not** by itself exclude `null`.
questions.explanation: `typeof null === "object"` is the classic trap; arrays also report `"object"`. You must add an explicit `x !== null` (and often `Array.isArray(x)`) check.

questions.question: Which built-in guard narrows a value to a class type such as `Date`, `Error`, or `RegExp` by checking its prototype chain?
options: `instanceof`
`typeof`
`in`
A user-defined `x is T` predicate
questions.explanation: `instanceof` narrows by prototype chain, ideal for classes and built-ins. `typeof` narrows primitives; `in` narrows by property presence; neither tests a constructor.

questions.question: What is the narrowed type of `value` at the point marked `(C)`?
options: `number`
`string | number`
`string | number | undefined`
`undefined`
questions.explanation: The first early return removes `undefined`; the second guard's branch returns for `string`, so the fall-through at (C) is whatever remains — `number`. Each guard peels one member off the union.

questions.question: For the `Result` type below, what happens with `return r.error.length` after the `if (r.ok) return r.value` guard?
options: It compiles: after the guard, `r` is narrowed to the `{ ok: false; error: string }` member
It errors: `error` is unreachable because `r` is still the full `Result` union
It errors: `ok` is a `boolean`, not a literal, so it cannot discriminate
It compiles only if you add an explicit `as` cast on the value of `r`
questions.explanation: `ok: true | false` is itself a discriminated union. After `if (r.ok) return`, the complement narrows `r` to the `ok: false` member, so `r.error.length` is valid. Writing `r.value` there would instead error.

questions.question: What is the inferred type of `cleaned`?
options: `string[]`
`(string | number | null)[]`
`unknown[]`
`(string | null)[]`
questions.explanation: `Array.prototype.filter` has an overload accepting a type predicate `(x: T) => x is S` and returning `S[]`. Because `isString` narrows to `string`, the element type is refined to `string`. A plain boolean-returning callback would leave the type unchanged.

questions.question: For `count: number | undefined`, you want a presence check that still treats `0` as valid data. Which condition is correct?
options: `if (count !== undefined)` — `0` takes the THEN branch as a valid number
`if (count)` — `0` takes the THEN branch as a valid number
`if (!!count)` — the coercion makes `0` truthy
`if (count || count === null)` — this keeps `0` as present
questions.explanation: `if (count)` (and `!!count`) sends `0` to the ELSE branch because `0` is falsy, silently treating real zero as absent. An explicit `count !== undefined` (or `count != null`) keeps `0` and `""` as data.

questions.question: How does an assertion function (`asserts x is T`) differ from a user-defined type guard (`x is T`) in terms of where narrowing applies?
options: The assertion narrows `x` for the rest of the current scope after the call; the guard narrows only inside the `if` that calls it
Both the assertion and the guard narrow only inside the calling `if` block
The assertion narrows only inside the calling `if`; the guard narrows the rest of the scope
The assertion narrows globally for every reference to `x`, including lines before the call
questions.explanation: A guard narrows within its `if` branch only. An assertion function either returns or throws, so after the call the value is known to be `T` for the remainder of the scope — no surrounding `if` needed.

questions.question: Under TS strict mode (`useUnknownInCatchVariables`, on by default in TS 6.0 strict), what is the type of `e` in `catch (e)`, and why?
options: `unknown`, because JavaScript can `throw` any value, so you must narrow before use
`any`, so that you can immediately access `e.message`
`Error`, because almost everything thrown is an `Error`
`never`, because the catch only runs on failure
questions.explanation: JS allows throwing any value (string, number, undefined), so the only sound assumption is `unknown`. You must narrow, e.g. `e instanceof Error`, before touching `.message`. Treating it as `any` reintroduces the unsafety the flag prevents.

questions.question: Inside the `if (a === b)` branch, what are the narrowed types of `a` and `b`?
options: Both `a` and `b` narrow to `string`
`a` stays `string | number`; `b` stays `string | boolean`
Both `a` and `b` narrow to `string | number | boolean`
Both narrow to `never` because the unions do not fully overlap
questions.explanation: Equality narrows two variables relative to each other to their common type. The only type both unions share is `string`, so inside the branch both `a` and `b` are `string`.

questions.question: Which statements about discriminated (tagged) unions are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: The discriminant property must be a literal type (e.g. `"loading"`, `200`, `true`), not a general `string`/`number`
`switch (x.kind)` narrows the whole object `x`, not just `x.kind`, so each case sees that member's unique fields
They pair naturally with a `never` exhaustiveness check so adding a member breaks the build until handled
A general `string`-typed tag works just as well for distinguishing the members
questions.explanation: The tag must be a distinct literal to discriminate; switching on it narrows the entire object; and the `never` exhaustiveness pattern makes a forgotten case a compile error. A general `string` tag can't distinguish members, so the last option is wrong.

questions.question: Which guard-to-job pairings are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `typeof` — narrows primitives by their runtime type string
`in` — narrows a union by the presence of a property
`instanceof` — narrows a plain object literal that structurally matches an interface
`instanceof` — narrows a value to a class/built-in via the prototype chain
questions.explanation: `typeof` handles primitives, `in` handles property presence, and `instanceof` handles classes/built-ins by prototype chain. `instanceof` does nothing for plain interface shapes — interfaces have no runtime constructor.

questions.question: What is the type of the `x` property in `type Conflict = { x: string } & { x: number }`?
options: `string & number`, which reduces to `never` — the property is unusable
`string | number`, since either branch could supply it
`string`, because the first operand listed wins
`unknown`, because the two declarations conflict
questions.explanation: For object intersections, overlapping property types are themselves intersected. `string & number` reduces to `never`, so you can never assign `Conflict.x` — a compile-time signal that the shapes clash.

questions.question: Why does adding a `triangle` member without a matching `case` make `default: return assertNever(s)` fail to compile?
options: In `default`, the reachable type of `s` is the unhandled `triangle` member, which is not assignable to `assertNever`'s `never` parameter
`assertNever` can only be called once per file, and `area` already exhausted it
A `switch` without a matching `case` is always a syntax error in strict mode
`s.kind` widens to `string` once three members exist, which breaks narrowing
questions.explanation: After handling circle and square, CFA leaves the triangle member as the reachable type in `default`. Passing a non-`never` value to a parameter typed `never` is an error, pointing exactly at the missing case.

questions.question: Which statements about how control-flow narrowing can be lost or limited are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Reassigning a variable resets its narrowing to the declared type (or the assigned value's type)
`const` locals stay narrowed longer than `let`, because the compiler knows they can't be reassigned
A variable captured and possibly mutated by a later closure call is conservatively widened back inside the closure
Once narrowed, a `let` variable keeps that narrowed type for the rest of the function even after reassignment
questions.explanation: Reassignment resets narrowing; `const` retains narrowing longer than `let`; and closures that may mutate a captured variable cause conservative widening. The last option is false — a `let` reassignment discards the prior narrowing.

questions.question: A user-defined type guard's predicate (`x is T`) is trusted by the compiler without verifying the function body, so a logically wrong implementation produces wrong narrowing with no runtime safety net.
questions.explanation: The predicate is a promise the compiler trusts. If the boolean logic doesn't actually prove the asserted type, callers get an incorrect narrowed type and there is no runtime check to catch it — the body and asserted type must stay in lockstep.
